title: Witch Week - Chapter 7 (Fragment 5)
text:
“Take me down to the matron in the sick bay,” Brian said.

It was a school rule that a person who felt ill had to find another person to take them to the matron. It had been made because, before that, the sick bay had been crowded with healthy people trying to get an afternoon off. The idea was that you could not deceive your friends. It did not work very well. Estelle Green, for instance, got Karen to take her to the matron at least twice a week. As far as Charles could see, Brian looked his usual pink and perky self, just like Estelle.

“You don’t look ill to me,” he said. He wanted to find Simon and see if the spell was working.

“How about this then?” said Brian. To Charles’s surprise, he suddenly turned pale. He stared vaguely at the wall, with one eye pointing inward slightly. “This is it,” Brian said. “Don’t I look rather as if I’d been hypnotized?”

“You look as if you’ve been hit on the head,” Charles said rudely. “Get Nirupam to take you.”

“He looked after me this morning,” said Brian. “I want as many witnesses as possible. I helped you last night. You help me now.”

“You didn’t help me last night,” said Charles.

“Yes, I did,” said Brian. “You came in and you went to sleep on the floor, just at the end of my bed. I got you in your bed. I even hooked your glasses on your bedrail for you.” And he looked at Charles, very meaningfully.

Charles stared back. Brian was so thin and small that it was hard to believe he could lift anyone into bed. But, whether it was true or not, Charles realized that Brian had gotten him over a barrel. He knew Charles had been up last night. He had caught him with Simon’s comb in his hands just now. Charles could not see why Brian wanted to go to the matron, but that was his own affair. “All right,” he said. “I’ll take you.”

Inside the bathroom, on the other side of the quadrangle, the girls crowded in around Nan. “Where’s Estelle?” asked Theresa.

“Outside, keeping watch,” said Karen. “That’s all she would do.”

“What’s this about?” Nan asked aggressively.

“We want you to do some proper witchcraft,” said Theresa. “Here, where we can see you. We’ve none of us seen any before. And we know you can. Come on. We won’t give you away.”

The other girls joined in. “Come on, Nan. We won’t tell.”

The bathroom was a very public one. There were six baths in it, in a row. As the girls all crowded forward, Nan backed away, down the space between two of the baths. This was evidently just what they had wanted. Delia said, “That’s it.” Heather said, “Fetch it out.” And Karen bent and pulled the groundsman’s old broom out from under the left-hand bath. Julia and Deborah seized it and propped it across the two baths in front of Nan, penning her in. Nan looked from it to them.

“We want you to get on it and fly about,” Theresa explained.

“Everyone knows that’s what witches do,” said Karen.

“We’re asking you very nicely,” said Theresa.

Typical Theresa double-think, Nan thought angrily. She was not asking her nicely. It was a smiling jeer. But if anyone asked Theresa afterward, she would say, with honest innocence, that she had been perfectly kind.

“We can prove you’re a witch anyway, if you won’t,” Theresa said kindly.

“Yes, everyone knows that witches don’t drown,” said Delia. “You can put them right under water and they stay alive.”

vocabulary.definition: A matter or concern; a particular event or situation.
vocabulary.example: Charles could not see why Brian wanted to go to the matron, but that was his own affair.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing readiness to attack or confront.
vocabulary.example: ” Nan asked aggressively.

vocabulary.definition: Containing a large number of people or things.
vocabulary.example: It had been made because, before that, the sick bay had been crowded with healthy people trying to get an afternoon off.

vocabulary.definition: To cause someone to believe something that is not true.
vocabulary.example: The idea was that you could not deceive your friends.

vocabulary.definition: To die by being underwater and unable to breathe.
vocabulary.example: “Yes, everyone knows that witches don’t drown,” said Delia.

vocabulary.definition: To put someone into a state resembling sleep in which they are more easily influenced.
vocabulary.example: “Don’t I look rather as if I’d been hypnotized?

vocabulary.definition: The state of being not guilty or free from moral wrong.
vocabulary.example: But if anyone asked Theresa afterward, she would say, with honest innocence, that she had been perfectly kind.

vocabulary.definition: To make rude and mocking remarks, usually in a loud voice.
vocabulary.example: It was a smiling jeer.

vocabulary.definition: In a kind or gentle manner.
vocabulary.example: “We can prove you’re a witch anyway, if you won’t,” Theresa said kindly.

vocabulary.definition: A woman in charge of domestic and medical arrangements in an institution, especially a hospital.
vocabulary.example: “Take me down to the matron in the sick bay,” Brian said.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that has a serious or important meaning.
vocabulary.example: ” And he looked at Charles, very meaningfully.

vocabulary.definition: Having a light or washed-out color due to illness, fear, or shock.
vocabulary.example: To Charles’s surprise, he suddenly turned pale.

vocabulary.definition: Cheerful and lively.
vocabulary.example: As far as Charles could see, Brian looked his usual pink and perky self, just like Estelle.

vocabulary.definition: Supported or held in position by placing against something.
vocabulary.example: Julia and Deborah seized it and propped it across the two baths in front of Nan, penning her in.

vocabulary.definition: Open to or shared by all people.
vocabulary.example:
”

The bathroom was a very public one.

vocabulary.definition: A rectangular space or courtyard, especially one enclosed by buildings.
vocabulary.example:
”

Inside the bathroom, on the other side of the quadrangle, the girls crowded in around Nan.

vocabulary.definition: A room or area in a school, ship, or camp where sick people are cared for.
vocabulary.example: “Take me down to the matron in the sick bay,” Brian said.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magic charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: He wanted to find Simon and see if the spell was working.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is uncertain, indefinite, or unclear.
vocabulary.example: He stared vaguely at the wall, with one eye pointing inward slightly.

vocabulary.definition: A person, typically a woman, believed to have magical powers.
vocabulary.example: “Everyone knows that’s what witches do,” said Karen.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities.
vocabulary.example: “We want you to do some proper witchcraft,” said Theresa.

vocabulary.definition: A person who sees an event or action and can give a firsthand account.
vocabulary.example: “I want as many witnesses as possible.
